section_1_metadata.KANN_ownership: KANN is a joint venture between Xerxes Global Investment Ltd and CEC Africa Investments Ltd (both Zambian)
section_1_metadata.notable_event: CFO Andrew Atterbury died during the year; Ije Ikoku Okeke appointed interim CFO. Director John Albert Jones passed away April 2017.

revenue.note: Revenue growth of 7.5% — modest, largely from tariff structure not volume growth

cost_of_sales.note: Cost of energy purchased exceeded revenue collected — structurally loss-making at gross level

gross_loss.note: Gross loss nearly QUADRUPLED year-on-year. Every unit of electricity sold at a loss.

finance_costs_net.note: Finance costs more than doubled — interest on unpaid NBET/ONEM bills alone was N26.3bn

loss_for_year.note: Loss increased 60% year-on-year. Fifth consecutive year of losses since privatisation (2013).

total_liabilities.note: Liabilities grew 60.8% in one year — catastrophic deterioration

total_equity.note: Equity swung from -N11bn to -N86bn in one year. Company technically insolvent.

accumulated_deficit.note: Accumulated deficit grew from N29.6bn to N105.6bn — tripled in one year

prepaid.note: Prepaid share growing — from 17.8% to 20.9% year-on-year. Vitics signal: prepaid migration accelerating as customers seek control over spending.

unbilled_receivables_included_in_revenue.note: Revenue includes N3.53bn of estimated unbilled consumption — significant estimation risk

section_3_revenue_analysis.revenue_quality_assessment: LOW. Revenue growth driven by tariff structure, not more electricity delivered. Massive cost overrun means every N1 of revenue costs N1.29 to generate.

cost_of_energy_purchased.note: Energy purchase cost exceeded total revenue — company buys electricity for more than it collects

impairment_on_trade_receivables.note: Even with reduced impairment charge, cumulative allowance reached N47.75bn

operations_and_management_fee_to_KANN.note: O&M fee = 2% of net cash collected, paid to parent KANN. Related party — governance concern.

interest_on_NBET_ONEM_bills.note: CRITICAL: Interest charges on unpaid power purchase bills jumped 141% in one year. NIBOR+10% penalty rate creates a debt spiral — the less AEDC pays, the faster the debt grows.

property_plant_equipment_net.note: PPE increased — revaluation surplus of N913m recorded at year end (land/buildings revalued)

trade_receivables_gross.note: Gross receivables FELL despite revenue growth — suggests write-offs or impairments consuming the balance

impairment_allowance_on_receivables.note: Cumulative impairment now EXCEEDS gross trade receivables balance — the company has written off more than it currently holds

receivables_aging_2017.note: Pattern shifted dramatically from 2015. In 2015, 81% was 120+ days overdue. In 2017 the 120+ days bucket is only 17.5% but 0-30 day bucket has grown — suggests more recent customers defaulting faster.

cash_and_equivalents.note: Cash improved — CBN NEMSF drawdown of N20.23bn post year-end helped

NBET_ONEM_trade_payables.note: CRITICAL: Unpaid power purchase bills grew by N69.8bn (64%) in one year. This represents 91% of total liabilities. Company survives by not paying its energy supplier.

CAPMI_loan_balance.note: Customers advance-funded their own meters (CAPMI). Balance declining as meters are installed and refunded via energy units.

section_6_going_concern.directors_going_concern_assessment: Directors believe company will remain a going concern based on PSRP commitments from FGN
key_risks_cited: Loss of N75.98bn after tax in 2017
Current liabilities exceed current assets by N173.16bn
Total liabilities exceed total assets by N86.10bn
Non-cost-reflective tariffs are the structural root cause
PSRP implementation not within company's control
91% of liabilities owed to NBET/ONEM — debt spiral via NIBOR+10% interest
mitigating_factors_cited: FGN issued N701bn Payment Assurance Guarantee (PAG) in April 2017
FGN committed to fund sector deficits 2018-2021
FGN to pay MDA debts and verify 2013-2014 MDA billings
PSRP tariff trajectory to cost-reflective tariffs over 5 years
CBN NEMSF first tranche of N20.23bn received post year-end
section_6_going_concern.vitics_assessment: Going concern language is now standard boilerplate for all DISCOs. The real signal is the PACE of deterioration — equity went from -N11bn to -N86bn in 12 months. The PSRP was announced but its implementation was already behind schedule in 2017.

section_7_tariff_and_regulatory.settlement_mechanism: Net off against NBET/ONEM payables — timing under negotiation

interest_writeoff_pending.status: Directors believe interest will be written off in 2018
interest_writeoff_pending.note: This represents accumulated interest and penalties on market debts — not yet agreed with NERC

section_7_tariff_and_regulatory.cost_reflective_tariff_status: NOT ACHIEVED as of 2017 — identified as the major issue in the sector per PSRP
section_7_tariff_and_regulatory.PSRP_approved: April 2017 by FGN, World Bank backed
PSRP_key_commitments_relevant_to_tariff: Implement end-user tariff trajectory to cost-reflective over 5 years

section_8_metering_and_collection.metering_gap: Significant — majority of postpaid customers unmetered as at 2017

CAPMI_scheme.description: Credited Advance Payment for Metering Implementation — customers pre-fund their own meters
CAPMI_scheme.note: CAPMI refunds exceeded new installation income — scheme maturing, more refunds than new take-up

vendor_financed_mass_metering.contract_2_tenor: 36 months at 12% for first 2 years, 15% year 3
vendor_financed_mass_metering.note: Company borrowed from vendors to install meters — reflects inability to self-fund infrastructure

super_vendor_digital_payment.note: Digital/online payment infrastructure expanding — reduces cash handling, improves collection velocity

collection_strategy: KPIs for employees tied to collections

events.event: KANN declared force majeure in April 2015
events.note: KANN's force majeure in 2015 was referenced in aedc_2015.json. By 2017 this had been withdrawn but its consequences — KANN's financial exposure and Zambian management entrenchment — continued.

force_majeure_history.status_2017: No new force majeure declarations in 2017

cbn_intervention_fund.facility_name: CBN Nigerian Electricity Market Stabilisation Fund (NEMSF)
cbn_intervention_fund.purpose: Settle outstanding payment obligations to market participants during interim rules period, and legacy debts of PHCN generation companies owed to gas suppliers (transferred to NELMCO)
cbn_intervention_fund.status: AEDC had not drawn down during 2017 — received first tranche of N20.23bn post year-end (early 2018)
cbn_intervention_fund.collateral_requirement: Letter of credit — company borrowed N6.5bn from TCN and N1.21bn from NELMCO to collateralise letter of credit in favour of NBET; letter of credit obtained at N10.5bn from financial institution
cbn_intervention_fund.vitics_note: The CBN fund was first expected in 2015 (aedc_2015.json). Two years later it still had not been received during the reporting year. When it finally arrived (N20.23bn of N27bn expected), it was used to pay down NBET/ONEM debt, not to improve service.

nelmco_transition_liabilities.pre_completion_receivables_transferred_t: All trade receivables as at 31 October 2013
nelmco_transition_liabilities.pre_completion_liabilities_transferred_t: All contingent liabilities as at 31 October 2013
nelmco_transition_liabilities.outstanding_dispute: AEDC and NELMCO yet to agree on individual trade debtors and liabilities as at 2017 — unresolved since 2013
nelmco_transition_liabilities.NELMCO_payment_demand: NELMCO requested immediate payment of the N2.17bn provision — classified as current liability
nelmco_transition_liabilities.note: NELMCO transition liabilities remain unresolved 4 years post-privatisation. This legal/financial ambiguity is a permanent overhang.

customer_contributed_assets.note: No new customer-contributed assets in 2017. The practice of customers self-funding transformers and network infrastructure has effectively paused — possibly because customers are now investing in solar instead.
customer_contributed_assets.vitics_signal: The ABSENCE of customer-contributed assets in 2017 is a transition signal. In 2015 and 2016 consumers were still willing to fund grid infrastructure hoping service would improve. By 2017 that hope was fading — this is the year YouTube solar content in Nigeria became searchable at scale.

section_10_grid_defection_signals.score_rationale: Score upgraded from 3/10 (2015) to 5/10 (2017). Conditions for defection have materially worsened.

prepaid_migration.interpretation: Prepaid adoption is a behavioural precursor to solar — consumers want control over their energy spending. Same psychology drives solar adoption.

customer_contributed_assets_stopped.interpretation: Consumers have stopped funding grid infrastructure. The implied signal: they no longer believe grid investment will pay off.

GENCOS_eligible_customer_regulation.description: NERC enacted regulation allowing eligible customers to purchase directly from generation companies, bypassing DISCOs
GENCOS_eligible_customer_regulation.AEDC_response: Directors assessed probability of losing customers as low in short-medium term due to logistics required
GENCOS_eligible_customer_regulation.vitics_note: AEDC is worried about this regulation. It is an early form of energy choice — the regulatory precursor to distributed energy.

revenue_not_growing_with_tariff.interpretation: Energy purchased growing faster than energy billed — ATC&C losses worsening OR customers reducing consumption (demand destruction / self-generation)

going_concern_fifth_consecutive_year.interpretation: The grid is not improving. Five consecutive years of losses with worsening equity means no capital available for network rehabilitation. Poor supply quality drives off-grid adoption.

what_consumers_were_experiencing_in_2017.estimated_average_supply_hours: Not disclosed in report — typical FCT supply hours estimated at 8-12 hours/day based on industry reports
what_consumers_were_experiencing_in_2017.tariff_pressure: Tariff shortfall of N61.37bn built up 2015-2017 — NERC and FGN knew cost-reflective tariffs had not been achieved
what_consumers_were_experiencing_in_2017.billing_complaints: Estimated billing still dominant — consumers being billed for electricity not consumed, or amounts not matching actual usage
what_consumers_were_experiencing_in_2017.CAPMI_self_funding_continues: Consumers still funding their own meters via CAPMI — paying twice (paying for meter PLUS paying for electricity)
what_consumers_were_experiencing_in_2017.vitics_consumer_voice: 2017 is the year Nigerian consumers began comparing solar inverter costs to their electricity bills seriously. The CAPMI scheme — where you pre-fund your own meter — is psychologically very close to buying a solar panel. Same mental model, different technology.

PSRP_overview.approved: April 2017 by FGN, World Bank backed
key_financial_interventions: N701bn Payment Assurance Guarantee (PAG) issued to ensure NBET pays GENCOS
FGN committed to fund sector deficits 2018-2021
FGN paying MDA debts — MDAs owed N9.1bn recovered in 2017 via NBET net-off
MDA cash collections averaging 78% of monthly billings in 2017
Tariff trajectory to cost-reflective tariffs over 5 years
PSRP_overview.implementation_status_2017: Commenced but incomplete. Tariff cost-reflective not yet implemented. MDA payments improving. CBN fund not yet received during year.

off_grid_reference_in_PSRP.quote_summary: PSRP included 'Implementation of off-grid renewable solutions to increase energy access' as one of its key initiatives
off_grid_reference_in_PSRP.vitics_significance: This is the FGN formally acknowledging off-grid solar as part of the solution — not just a stopgap. This language in an official recovery plan signals policy legitimacy for solar, even while the grid DISCOs are collapsing financially.

loss_for_year_trend.note: Losses have compounded every single year since privatisation. Revenue grew 82% from 2013-2017. Losses grew 451% over the same period. The business model is structurally broken.

net_assets_trend.note: Net assets destroyed completely by 2016, now deeply negative. The privatisation has destroyed all shareholder value within 4 years.

section_12_five_year_trajectory.vitics_five_year_narrative: Between 2013 and 2017, AEDC went from net assets of N85.8bn to negative N86.1bn — a destruction of N172bn of balance sheet value in 4 years. This is the financial story running parallel to Nigeria's solar adoption story. As AEDC's ability to deliver collapsed, consumers found alternatives.

section_13_consumer_impact.score_rationale: Score upgraded from 7/10 (2015) to 8/10 (2017). The financial deterioration has directly worsened consumer experience.
direct_consumer_harms: Estimated billing persists — consumers billed without meters, amounts disputed
CAPMI scheme — consumers paying to self-fund their own meters
Cost-reflective tariffs not implemented but tariff shortfall accumulating — future tariff shock guaranteed
Network maintenance starved of capital — infrastructure deteriorating
Aggressive disconnection policy — consumers disconnected when they cannot pay disputed bills
Five years of losses means no investment in supply quality improvement

indirect_transition_terms: I no go pay AEDC bill

section_14_vitics_analysis_instructions.document_role_in_vitics: AEDC 2017 is the financial evidence for why Abuja consumers accelerated solar adoption in 2017-2019. It confirms the financial collapse was not new news in 2020 — it had been building since 2013.
key_signals_for_ai_analysis: Use the five-year trajectory to show structural not cyclical failure
The PSRP off-grid language is a policy legitimacy signal for solar
Prepaid migration (17.8% → 20.9%) is a solar behavioural precursor
Customer contributed assets going to zero is a grid trust collapse signal
NBET/ONEM debt of N179bn (86% of liabilities) explains why the grid cannot be maintained — no capital flows back into infrastructure
The GENCOS eligible customer regulation is an early energy choice signal
CBN NEMSF fund — expected in 2015, arrived partially in early 2018 — shows government interventions are always late and partial

continuity_signals: Going concern: flagged in 2015, still flagged in 2017
CBN fund: expected 2015, still not received during 2017
CAPMI: active in 2015, still active in 2017 — metering gap not solved
Estimated billing: a complaint in 2015, still a KPMG key audit matter in 2017
Losses: N41.97bn in 2015 → N75.98bn in 2017 — accelerating not stabilising
KANN force majeure (2015) → withdrawn (2016) → Zambian management still in seat (2017)
what_changed_2015_to_2017: Equity went from positive N36.4bn (2015) to negative N86.1bn (2017)
Accumulated deficit went from N17.8bn surplus (2015) to N105.6bn deficit (2017)
PSRP launched (2017) — new government intervention framework
Off-grid officially acknowledged in government recovery plan (2017)

section_14_vitics_analysis_instructions.vitics_core_lens_application: The 2017 report answers the question: what were consumers experiencing while solar YouTube content was becoming searchable in Nigeria? Answer — fifth year of losses, going concern risk, estimated billing still unresolved, own meters self-funded, grid deteriorating. The financial statements are the explanation for the search behaviour Vitics tracks.
section_14_vitics_analysis_instructions.transition_year_assessment: 2017 is a BRIDGE year — the financial collapse is well advanced but the solar adoption surge has not yet peaked. The PSRP gives the appearance of government action while the underlying numbers worsen. Consumers in 2017 are at the tipping point between hoping the grid improves and deciding to leave it.

section_15_document_quality.method: Full visual read of all 62 pages of scanned PDF
section_15_document_quality.data_completeness: Full financial statements, notes, five-year summary, value added statement all present
section_15_document_quality.auditor_opinion_type: Unmodified with Material Uncertainty — Going Concern emphasis paragraph

section_16_transition_marker.transition_note: AEDC 2017 confirms the financial collapse that was driving consumer solar adoption. The PSRP off-grid acknowledgement is a policy legitimacy signal. Prepaid migration and customer contributed assets going to zero are behavioural defection signals. This is the last year before Solar Power Naija (2020) and the watershed of 2023 — it documents the conditions that made those events inevitable.
